1: A direct type: 'If his offering is a sacrifice of peace offering [zebach shelamim], if he offers an animal from the herd, male or female, he shall offer it without blemish [tamim] before YHWH.' The shelamim (peace offering, from shalom) uniquely features the offerer eating from the sacrifice in communion with God and the priests. Christ is our peace: 'He himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility' (Ephesians 2:14). The communion meal of the peace offering is the type of the Lord's Supper — restored fellowship between God and man at the table.
2: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on the head of his offering and kill it at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and Aaron's sons the priests shall throw the blood against the sides of the altar.' Hand-laying (semichah) appears again — the peace offering also requires identification, not just gratitude. The worshiper approaches not on the basis of his own worthiness but through substitutionary identification. Christ is the one who makes sinners acceptable to draw near (Hebrews 10:22: 'let us draw near with a true heart').
3: A direct type: 'And from the sacrifice of the peace offering, as a food offering to YHWH, he shall offer the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat portions (the richest, most energetic parts of the animal) are given entirely to YHWH — not eaten, not given to priests. The richest belongs to God alone. Christ gave what was most precious: 'Though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself' (Philippians 2:6–7) — the ultimate giving of the richest for God's purpose.
4: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The internal anatomy given to God — what is hidden, internal, private. Christ's self-giving was internal and total: 'not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42). The kidneys in Hebrew thought (kelayot) were the seat of the inner life; giving them to God types the complete inner surrender of Gethsemane.
5: A shadow: 'Then Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar on top of the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire; it is a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The peace offering burns on top of the burnt offering — it presupposes the olah beneath it. Peace with God (shelamim) rests on the foundation of total consecration (olah). Christ first consecrated himself entirely (John 17:19: 'I sanctify myself') and then made peace through the blood of his cross (Colossians 1:20).
6: A direct type: 'If his offering for a peace offering to YHWH is an animal from the flock, male or female, he shall offer it without blemish.' The peace offering uniquely accepts male or female — in this offering, the gender distinction of the olah (male only) is removed. This anticipates Paul's declaration: 'There is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). The peace Christ makes is equally available to all.
7: A direct type: 'If he offers a lamb for his offering, then he shall offer it before YHWH, lay his hand on the head of his offering, and kill it in front of the tent of meeting.' The lamb peace offering sets up the central NT image: Christ as 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). The hand-laying at the peace offering means the worshiper's need for peace — his estrangement from God — is transferred to the lamb.
8: A direct type: 'Aaron's sons shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.' Blood-throwing at the altar marks the peace offering as still fundamentally an atonement rite — fellowship with God is possible only through blood-mediated reconciliation. Colossians 1:20: God was pleased 'through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.'
9: A shadow: 'Then from the sacrifice of the peace offering he shall offer as a food offering to YHWH its fat; he shall remove the whole fat tail, cut off close to the backbone, and the fat that covers the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat tail (ha-alyah) — unique to broad-tailed sheep — is given entirely to YHWH. What is characteristically unique to the animal, its distinguishing feature, is offered to God. Christ offered what was uniquely his — his divine-human nature, the one thing no one else could give.
10: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' Repeated from v4 for the sheep variety — the internal organs that represent the hidden life. Christ's offering was not merely external conduct but the hidden obedience of his inner life: 'he learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8).
11: A direct type: 'And the priest shall burn it on the altar as a food offering to YHWH.' The peace offering is lechem isheh — 'food of the fire offering' or 'food offering.' God receives the sacrifice as nourishment — a figure of speech for acceptable worship. Christ calls himself the bread of life (John 6:35) and at the Eucharist, his body and blood are given as food: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). The communion meal of the peace offering becomes the Lord's Supper.
12: A shadow: 'If his offering is a goat, then he shall offer it before YHWH.' The third animal option — goat. The broad inclusivity of the peace offering (cattle, sheep, goat) mirrors the universality of the peace Christ makes: 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2).
13: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on its head and kill it in front of the tent of meeting, and the sons of Aaron shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.' The third repetition of hand-laying in chapter 3 (for cattle v2, sheep v7, goat v13). The triple repetition across all three animal types establishes identification/substitution as an absolute requirement — no peace offering without hand-laying, no peace with God without Christ bearing what we are.
14: A shadow: 'Then he shall offer from it, as his offering for a food offering to YHWH, the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' Fat portions again — the richest given to God. The goat's fat portions are identical to the cattle's and sheep's: the same principle governs all. The offering of the best to God is universal.
15: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The third occurrence of the kidney formula (vv4, 10, 15) — one for each animal type. The inner organs given to God type the complete inner surrender Christ made. Hebrews 10:7 applies Ps 40 to Christ: 'I have come to do your will, O God' — not outer compliance but the deepest inner obedience.
16: A direct type: 'And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a food offering with a pleasing aroma. All fat is YHWH's.' Kol-chelev laYHWH — 'all fat is YHWH's.' This is the absolute principle underlying the peace offering: the best, the richest, belongs to God alone. Christ's sacrifice was the offering of what was most precious to God — his own Son (Romans 8:32: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all'). The 'all fat is YHWH's' rule means nothing was withheld: 'he loved me and gave himself for me' (Galatians 2:20).
17: A direct type: 'It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations, in all your dwelling places, that you eat neither fat nor blood.' The double prohibition — no fat, no blood — is the peace offering's lasting theological boundary. Blood cannot be consumed because blood = life, and life belongs to God; it must be given to God (v16's kol-chelev laYHWH). Christ's blood cannot simply be possessed or managed by humanity — it must be received as the gift of the one who owned it: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' (Matthew 26:28). The no-blood-consumption rule prevents Israel from thinking of sacrifice as magic they control; it must be received as grace.
